title: Sheinbaum Unveils Expanded Plan México: 18 New Programs at National Museum of Anthropology
detail: President Sheinbaum announced 18 new programs and actions under Plan México at the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City, framing the expansion as a direct response to global trade realignment following Trump's broader reciprocal tariff announcements (from which Mexico was notably exempt under USMCA). The expanded plan emphasized Mexico's strategic position as a nearshoring hub: the 2026–2030 Infrastructure Investment Plan totals MX$5.6 trillion, with MX$722 billion allocated for 2026 alone. Sheinbaum highlighted the museum as a symbol of Mexico's sovereign identity and culture while positioning Plan México as the economic platform to benefit from U.S. manufacturing reshoring opportunities. Mexico's exemption from the latest round of U.S. reciprocal tariffs — due to USMCA compliance — was presented as validation of Sheinbaum's strategy of diplomatic cooperation without conceding sovereignty on trade terms.

title: US Section 301 Tariff Probe Comment Period Active; Mexico Prepares Defense Before April 15 Deadline
detail: The USTR's Section 301 trade investigation docket — opened March 17 after the February 2026 Supreme Court ruling struck down IEEPA tariffs — entered active comment period, with a Mexican government response due by April 15, 2026. The Section 301 process targets 'structural excess capacity' in manufacturing sectors including aluminum, automobiles, batteries, chemicals, electronics, semiconductors, and steel. Mexico's inclusion comes alongside 15+ other economies. A separate Section 301(b) forced-labor probe covers 60 economies. If USTR recommendations are adopted after the May 5 congressional hearing, new tariffs could be in place by late July 2026 — coinciding with the USMCA July 1 review deadline. Economy Secretary Ebrard's team was preparing submissions arguing Mexico's manufacturing sector meets USMCA rules of origin and does not constitute the 'excess capacity' described in the probe.

title: Cabinet Reshuffle: de la Fuente Exits Foreign Ministry; Roberto Velasco Named New Canciller
detail: Sheinbaum announced at her last pre-Semana Santa mañanera that Foreign Affairs Secretary Juan Ramón de la Fuente was leaving the cabinet citing health reasons following spinal surgery in late 2025. He was replaced by Roberto Velasco Álvarez, 38, who had served as Undersecretary for North America — making him one of the architects of Mexico's USMCA and tariff diplomacy. Velasco is closely associated with Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard and had been Ebrard's spokesperson during Trump's first term. Velasco holds a law degree from Universidad Iberoamericana and a master's degree from the University of Chicago. His appointment required Senate ratification. The change consolidates Ebrard's influence over both trade and foreign policy. Separately, Sheinbaum announced Héctor Alonso Romero Gutiérrez as the new director of Mexico's National Customs Agency (ANAM), tasked with strengthening revenue collection and digitizing port and land customs operations.

title: Sheinbaum Reports Mexico Achieves Diesel Self-Sufficiency, Covers 80% of Gasoline Needs Domestically
detail: At her April 1 mañanera, President Sheinbaum announced that Mexico has reached self-sufficiency in diesel production and now covers approximately 80% of its national gasoline needs domestically, citing output from the Dos Bocas (Olmeca) refinery in Tabasco. The announcement came despite the March 24, 2026 explosion and oil spill at Dos Bocas, which triggered a federal emergency response and FGR investigation. Sheinbaum also announced a 15,000-peso support payment to Veracruz fishermen affected by the earlier oil spill via the BienPesca program, and confirmed the price cap on diesel for transport carriers at 28.28 pesos per liter would continue despite rising global oil prices.
